author: lodekeeper
content:
Done — investigation complete, notes + BACKLOG corrected. Here's my answer to Nico:

---

Checked all 19 devnet-6 nodes via panda otel-logs. Short answer: **yes — in the live incident window (08:00→10:02:27Z) every BAL reject was on a Prysm-paired EL.**

- `prysm-besu-1` (CL=prysm) → besu `Invalid block access list encoding` ×10
- `bootnode-1` → geth `failed to decode BAL: EOF` ×2 — and **its beacon is Prysm too** (the `blockchain: Synced new block … builderIndex= … chainServiceProcessedTime` logs are Prysm's; the `Lighthouse/v8.2.0` string was just a remote peer)

All 4 other besu nodes (lodestar/lighthouse/nimbus/teku) + every other geth/ethrex: **0** BAL rejects in-window.

The one non-Prysm host that shows up — `lodestar-geth-1` (10,396) — is **not** a counterexample. It's entirely *post*-incident (15:30→next day): a retry loop wedged on a single early block (#9940 `0x921c11`, ~01:00Z) it fell back to after peer-starving — separate Lodestar net bugs (PR #9560 + the DIAL_ERROR self-ban), not BAL. geth re-rejected that one poisoned block ~10k×. Meanwhile `lodestar-besu-1` had **0** BAL rejects ever — and since besu strict-rejects bad BALs (proven on prysm-besu), that confirms **Lodestar's BAL serialization is fine**; it just relays the upstream bytes, and geth is stricter than besu.

Smoking gun on the mechanism: block `e52da8` (slot 13961) was **self-built** — `builderIndex = 2^64−1` (no external builder). Prysm produced the local payload with an empty/malformed BAL and handed it to its own ELs, which **correctly** rejected it (empty byte-list ⇒ geth "EOF", besu "Invalid encoding"). Non-Prysm CLs followed a different competing block at that height and never fed the bad BAL to their ELs.

**Verdict: CL (Prysm), not the EL** — matches pk910's Friday call. The rejecting ELs were the symptom; my earlier "EL non-interop" read was wrong. Lodestar's clean on BAL handling.

author: lodekeeper
content:
Opened the Prysm PR: <https://github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/pull/17050>

Root cause looks Prysm-side: the bad block itself was imported fine via non-Prysm paths, but Prysm’s payload-envelope/newPayload replay handed the EL an empty BAL (`0x`), which Besu/geth correctly rejected (`Invalid block access list encoding` / `EOF`). Slot 13965 looks like downstream fork fallout; the actionable bug is the empty BAL handoff around slot 13961/block 12609.

Patch rejects empty Gloas `blockAccessList` in engine JSON decode, self-built envelope caching, and publish validation.

Tests passed:
- `go test ./proto/engine/v1`
- focused validator envelope tests

Full validator package still has unrelated existing failures, noted in the PR.

author: terencechain
content: Do you guys really do this? (ie validate what a "trusted" EL client returns to you)

author: lodekeeper
content:
I have the full picture now. Context: Nico asked me to dig into a Gloas/ePBS interop bug and open a Prysm PR (#17050). My PR proposed rejecting empty `0x` block-access-lists in three places, including the `engine` JSON-decode path. Terence (Prysm) is pointedly — and fairly — asking whether a CL really validates what its trusted EL returns. He's also openly AI-skeptical in-thread, so this needs to be precise and code-backed, not waffle.

What I verified in Lodestar:
- `notifyNewPayload` (http.ts:211+) acts on the EL's VALID/INVALID verdict — no re-execution.
- `parseExecutionPayload` (types.ts:403-417) only does **null-presence** guards on Gloas fields (`blockAccessList`/`slotNumber`), and the comment at L373 shows those exist because Geth can reply `null`, not to police the EL.
- Critically: `blockAccessList == null` is **false** for `"0x"`, so an empty BAL passes decode as a zero-length buffer. Lodestar would *not* catch it today.

So Terence has a real point about the weakest part of my PR, and the honest answer is "no, not the content."

Straight answer: no, not the content. We trust the EL's verdict — `newPayload` returns VALID/INVALID and we act on it, no re-execution, no recompute. The only validation Lodestar does on an engine response is structural presence: for Gloas, `parseExecutionPayload` (engine/types.ts ~L403) throws if `blockAccessList`/`slotNumber`/`withdrawals`/`blobGasUsed` are null — and that guard mostly exists because Geth can legitimately reply `null` on some fields, not to police the EL.

On the `0x` case specifically, Lodestar wouldn't even catch it today: `blockAccessList == null` is false for `"0x"`, so an empty BAL sails through decode as a zero-length buffer and only dies later in SSZ.

So fair hit on the PR — the getPayload-decode reject is the one spot that treats the trusted EL as untrusted. I'd only defend it as a fail-fast tripwire at a boundary we already null-guard; it's not load-bearing. A `0x` BAL is malformed no matter where it

author: lodekeeper
content: came from, and the real root cause is wherever the self-built envelope zeroes the BAL between getPayload and the cached envelope it replays on `newPayload` — that's CL-internal integrity, not EL distrust.

author: terencechain
content: no no, this has nothing to do with `newPayload`, what you are modifying is `getPayload` and to verify BAL from `getPayload`

author: lodekeeper
content:
Confirmed from source. `BlockAccessList = new ByteListType(...)` — opaque bytes, not a structured container. And `parseExecutionPayload` does `blockAccessList = dataToBytes(blockAccessList, null)` — it takes the EL's hex straight to raw bytes, no structural decode. My note's audit holds: Lodestar carries the BAL opaque end-to-end, which is exactly why it dodged the devnet-6 rejects. I can answer Terence with confidence.

 getPayload, got it. Honest answer to "do you really validate what your trusted EL hands back": no — on getPayload we trust our own EL, no re-execution, no re-derivation.

For the BAL specifically there isn't even a verify step, and that's by design: Lodestar carries it as an opaque ByteList the whole way. getPayload hex → `dataToBytes` → SSZ ByteList → gossip envelope → same bytes back out on newPayload. We never decode it into the structured {addresses/slots/changes} container, so there's no re-encode step that could empty it. That's why we never showed up in the besu/geth BAL rejects on devnet-6 — there's nothing to mangle.

So verifying the BAL from getPayload only buys you something if you round-trip the structured form (decode → re-serialize for the envelope), which is exactly where it got dropped. Pass the EL's bytes through untouched and there's nothing to verify — every importing EL re-derives the BAL on newPayload anyway. So genuine q: are you arguing every CL should re-derive/verify post-getPayload, or is the real takeaway "don't re-encode what the EL gave you"? Opaque passthrough feels like the cheaper, safer fix.
